testing_procedures: 3 Description of Scope of Work and Approach Taken 3.1 Assessors Validation of Defined Scope Accuracy Describe how the assessor validated the accuracy of the defined PCI DSS scope for the assessment: As noted in Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Requirements and Testing Procedures: The minimum steps for an entity to confirm the accuracy of their PCI DSS scope are specified in PCI DSS Requirement 12.5.2. The entity is expected to retain documentation to show how PCI DSS scope was determined. The documentation is retained for assessor review and for reference during the entitys next PCI DSS scope confirmation activity. For each PCI DSS assessment, the assessor validates that the scope of the assessment is accurately defined and documented. Describe how the assessors evaluation of scope differs from the assessed Not Applicable entitys evaluation of scope as documented in Requirement 12.5. If no difference was identified, mark as Not Applicable. Provide the name of the assessor who attests that: Ron White  They have performed an independent evaluation of the scope of the assessed entitys PCI DSS environment.  If the assessors evaluation identified areas of scope not included in the assessed entitys documented scope, the assessed entity has updated their scoping documentation.  The scope of the assessment is complete and accurate to the best of the assessors knowledge. Describe any business functions, locations, payment channels, or other areas This assessment only covers the Shopify Merchant  Service Provider of scope that were excluded from the assessment including the following: environment. All other Shopify business units are out of scope for this  What was excluded. assessment  Why was it excluded.  If it was included in a separate assessment. If none, mark as Not Applicable. Identify any factors that resulted in reducing or limiting scope (for example, Not Applicable segmentation of the environment, use of a P2PE solution, etc.) If none, mark as Not Applicable. Note: An environment with no segmentation is considered a flat network where all systems are considered to be in scope.  If No, provide the name of the assessor who attests that the entire network Not Applicable has been included in the scope of the assessment.  If Yes, complete the following:  Describe how the segmentation is implemented, including the Incoming transaction connection requests from the internet flow through technologies and processes used. one of three GCP regions (Iowa, South Carolina) to a GCP load balancer with GCP network policies controlling access to the production internal node pools and a firewall restricting access to the MySQL databases. Outgoing transaction/authorizations flow from the GCP region external node pools through a GCP firewall to the payment gateways. Management connectivity is restricted by a GCP firewall to an OpenVPN server which permits access to the various services required to operate the environment. Description of Account Data Flows Identify in which of the following account data flows the assessed entity participates: Note: These data flows must be described in detail in the sections of the table that follow.  Authorization  Capture  Settlement  Chargeback/Dispute  Refunds  Other Identify and describe all data flows. Descriptions should include how and where account data enters the environment, is transmitted, is processed, is stored, and how and why any personnel access the account data. Add rows as necessary. Account data flows Description (For example, account data flow 1, (Include the type of account data) account data flow 2) Apay PAN Exchange dataflow 1. Amazon generates a JSON web token using a public key shared by to them by Shopify. They make a POST request to Cardsink containing the token. This endpoint is secured by mTLS. Shopify provided a client side certificate for mTLS to Amazon. 2. The token is stored in Memcached. Which is a volatile in-memory story. The tokens key in Memcached is a randomly generated session ID. 3. The session ID is returned to Amazon. 4. Amazon makes a POST request to Shopify with the session ID obtained from 1-3. 5. Shopify passes the session ID to Payment Service to vault the card. 6. Payment Service passes the session ID to Cardserver to vault the card in Cardserver. 7. Cardserver fetches the token from Memcached. 8. The tokens signature is verified and decrypted using the private key. 9. The card details are fetched from the token and are encrypted and stored in Cardservers datastore. Cardserver generates a random vault token for this credit card. 10. The vault token is returned to payment service. There is no cardholder data returned here. 11. Payment service returns the vault token back to Shopify. 12. Shopify passes the vault token amazon for them to store. Billing Dataflow  Billing Event Diagram This flow is triggered when a merchant is paying an invoice for a billing product. 1. A Billing event is triggered to pay an invoice. Billing calls payment service telling it to charge a merchant for some amount. PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 40

2. Payment Service fetches the vaulted card token that is associated with the merchant and creates a transaction on Cardserver with the vault card token. 3. Cardserver fetches the encrypted PAN from the database and decrypts it. 4. The PAN is sent to the Paymeny Gateway to process a payment. 5. The result from the Payment Gateway is returned to Cardserver, this includes no cardholder data, however, it will have a truncated PAN. 6. The data from the transaction is returned to Payment Service. (No CHD here) 7. The data from payment service is then returned to Shopify. Billing Dataflow Diagram  Merchant This flow is utilized when a merchant is picking a billing plan without an active payment method on file. Dataflow 1. A merchant selects subscription without an active payment method and is prompted to add a new card 2. Credit card fields (name, number, CVV, and expiry) are fetched from hosted-fields, and are rendered in iFrames. 3. The merchant submits their card details, which are sent in a POST request to Cardsink. A LUHN check, as well as a validation against the expiry is performed here before the merchant can submit the card details. 4. The card details are encrypted using Cardservers public key and are stored in Memcached. A session ID is randomly generated and is used as the identifier to look up the encrypted data that is stored in Memcached. 5. Memcached is a volatile in-memory cache, which also enforces a TTL of 1 hour for any items stored in the cache. 6. The session ID is returned to the merchants browser. 7. The session ID is sent to Shopify in a POST request to perform a verification on the credit card, which passes the session ID to payment service. 8. Payment Service calls into Shopifys Card Data Environment with the session ID to perform verification. 9. Cardserver kicks off the verification process with the request from payment service. 10. The session ID is used to fetch the encrypted card details from Memcached. These are then decrypted using Cardservers private key. 11. Cardserver sends the card details to the payment gateway to perform an Auth. 12. The response from the Auth is parsed and validated. 13. The card data is encrypted and stored in Cardservers database. The CVV of a credit card is not stored. 14. Cardserver stores information about the transaction to its database, and returns the transaction data back to Payment Service, which continues to pass the data back to Shopify. The transaction data includes a truncated PAN. PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 41

Card Updater Update Flow  Service This flow describes how Shopify receives card update notifications and processes those to receive the card Provider Dataflow details for credit cards which have been enrolled in Braintrees card updater service. This flow utilizes Braintrees Forward API, which allows Shopify to register an endpoint and data format to receive full card details from Braintree upon request. Braintree generates a file of information about the enrolled cards which have changed. This information includes the token to identify the card, and information about the kind of change. 1. Braintree makes a http request to Payment Service (webhook) to notify Shopify about this new file and its location. The payload of this request is signed by Braintree, and this signature is verified upon receipt. The data file is retrieved and processed in this way. For each card which has a relevant update (i.e., new expiry, new account number) 2,3. Payment Service makes an API request to Braintrees Forward API, requesting that the new card details be sent to the Cardsink API. This returns a session ID to the Payment Service. 4. Payment Service makes a request to Cardserver to update the card based on the session ID. Cardserver writes a new credit card record with the data in the Cardsink session. 4. The non-sensitive card details are returned to Payment Service. 5. Payment Service updates its credit card record with these details. Card Updater Vault Flow  Service This flow describes how cards are sent to Braintree to enroll in a card updater service, which notifies Shopify Provider Dataflow when the card details are changed and gives Shopify the opportunity to receive the new information. 1. A Shopify service, such as billing, requests that a credit card which is already vaulted for payment is enrolled to receive updates from Braintree. 2,3. The Payment Service (a Shopify service) sends a request to Cardserver to forward the card details to Braintree to enroll that card in Braintrees card updater service. Braintrees API returns a token representing the card in their systems, and card metadata describing details about the card. This is stored in Cardserver along with the existing card details and returned to the Payment Service. 4,5. The Payment Service also stores the Braintree token in the non-PCI credit card record it maintains. The card metadata is returned to the initiating client. Card Vaulting  New Card or Wallet  1. After checkout, an async job is dispatched that will initiate the card vaulting procedure Merchant Dataflow 2, 3. A request to vault the card is made to the Payment Service graphQL endpoint, containing the Session Token identifier and Receipt MIT data. The Payment Service will make a POST request across the Cardholder Data environment containing the information from the previous request. PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 42

3. Cardserver will request the Cardholder Data from the cache using the Session Token, and the decrypted card data will be passed back to the Cardserver. 4. The decrypted data is modeled and stored in the Cardserver database. 5. The cache entry containing the Cardholder data is erased. 6. A new public token identifier that points to the stored card is linked to the customer account, for use in future purchases. Checkout Dataflow  Service Provider This diagram depicts the dataflow for a credit card payment in a Shopify checkout. This flow goes through Dataflow SP France payment Service, which is only for Shopify Payments France. 1.The buyer adds items to a cart and navigates to the checkout page. 2. The credit card fields are retrieved from Hosted Fields and are rendered in iFrames on the checkout page. 2a. A check is made to see if the card number is valid using LUHN, along with a check of the cards expiry. If either validation fails, no forward movement is made. 3. The PAN, CVV, expiry, and name are sent in a POST request to Cardsink. 3a. The posted data is encrypted using the public key. 3b. A session ID is generated which will be used to reference the encrypted data in Memcached. 4. The encrypted data is stored in Memcached and is referenced by the session ID. Data stored in Memcached expires after 1 hour. 5. The session ID is returned to the buyers browser. The session ID does not contain any card holder data. 6. The session ID is sent in a POST request to Shopify. 6a. The session ID, along with the card and payment provider details are passed to Payment Service. 7. Payment Service creates a transaction by passing the data obtained from 6a to Cardserver which is inside the CDE. 8. Cardserver fetches the encrypted card details from Memcached using the session ID. 8a. Cardserver decrypts the card details using the private key. 8b. Cardserver deletes the encrypted card details from Memcached, thus making session IDs single use. 9. The PAN, CVV, expiry and name are sent to the payment provider to create an auth. 10. A truncated PAN, status of auth and identifiers for the Auth are returned to Cardserver. 11. The truncated PAN, identifiers, and status of auth are returned to Shopify from the CDE. Checkout Dataflow  Service Provider This diagram is depicting the dataflow for a credit card payment in a Shopify checkout except for SP France Dataflow 1. The buyer adds items to a cart and navigates to the checkout page. PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 43

2. The credit card fields are retrieved from Hosted Fields and are rendered in iFrames on the checkout page. 2a. A check is made to see if the card number is valid using LUHN, as well as a check of the cards expiry. If either validation fails, no forward movement is made. 3. The PAN, CVV, expiry, and name are sent in a POST request to Cardsink. 3a. The posted data is encrypted using the public key. 3b. A session ID is generated which will be used to reference the encrypted data in Memcached. 4. The encrypted data is stored in Memcached and is referenced by the session ID. Data stored in Memcached expires after 1 hour. 5. The session ID is returned to the buyers browser. The session ID does not contain any cardholder data. 6. The session ID is sent in a POST request to Shopify. 7. The session ID, along with the card and payment provider details are passed to Cardserver. 8. Cardserver fetches the encrypted card details from Memcached using the session ID. 8a. Cardserver decrypts the card details using the private key. 8b. Cardserver deletes the encrypted card details from Memcached, thus making session IDs single use. 9. The PAN, CVV, expiry and name are sent to the payment provider to create an auth. 10. A truncated PAN, status of auth and identifiers for the Auth are returned to Cardserver. 11. The truncated PAN, identifiers, and status of auth are returned to Shopify from our CDE. Card Billing  Google Pay  Merchant 1. A buyer initiates a checkout and selects Google Pay as a payment method provider within the Shopify App, Dataflow initiating the Google Pay JavaScript API and displaying the payment method selection sheet. 2. Upon selection of a payment method, an Elliptic Curve Integrated Encryption Scheme (ECIES) encrypted payload containing the users chosen payment data is sent to Shopify by Google. 3. This encrypted payload is assigned an identifier (the session token) for future read access within the Cardholder data environment (the CDE) and is forwarded to the CDE along with additional billing metadata. 4. The payload is decrypted using Shopify credentials in accordance with the ECIES encryption scheme. Keys rotate yearly. 5. The payload is temporarily cached  the session token and billing metadata are sent out of the CDE and back to the Shopify App for the User to finalize the transaction. 6. The transaction is finalized, and a charge is initiated, where the session token is passed back to the CDE to retrieve the cardholder data. 7. The cached entry is read using the session token. 8. The cached entry is deleted unless the session token has been marked as a vaulting candidate. A VaultingCandidate is a transient state whereby a user will have the opportunity to have their card vaulted for future use but has not been presented with the opportunity to confirm their intent. PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 44

9. The decrypted cardholder data is then passed out of the CDE via TLS/HTTPS to Stripe for processing, and the detail of the resulting transaction is passed back to the User. 10. An Order is built based on the transaction and charge result, which is presented to the User containing information about their purchase, and receipt, if applicable. Legacy Card Vaulting  New Card  This flow is triggered from the Checkout flow when a buyer opts in to save their card information. Service Provider Dataflow 1.A buyer opts into the feature by selecting the checkbox in checkout. 2. A customer record is created in Shop Pay 3. The checkout flow happens (See checkout flow for details). There is a conditional flag in the checkout flow that allows a card to be vaulted. If the card is a vaulting candidate, the session ID does not immediately get deleted. 4. Shopify tells Cardserver to vault the card that was previously used. 5. The card details are fetched from Memcached using the same session ID from the checkout flow and is decrypted. The card details are then encrypted and stored in Cardservers datastore. A vault card token is created for the card. The vault card token is returned to Shopify. 6 Shopify dispatches an event with the vault card token and checkout information. This event is consumed by ShopPay to complete the customer record. Network Tokenization for Credit Cards A partner is processing payments for credit cards held in the Shop Pay wallet. The flow is triggered when a buyer returned by Shop Pay  A Shop Pay user is purchasing a product from a partner and the payment method they choose is Shop Pay. The buyer is uses stored payment method on a Partners redirected to Shop Pay to login. Upon logging in and selecting their payment method, they are redirected back to shop (non-Shopify Merchant) the partner. On the redirect back, the partner is passed an access token. 1. The partner calls the confirm order API with the access token that was obtained from the redirect, along with a public key that will be used for encryption. 2. Shop Pay maps the access token to a vaulted card token, calls into Shopifys Card Data Environment with the vault card token and the public key to fetch the card data. 3. The credit card is fetched from Shopifys data store using the vaulted card token. From here there are two different paths, depending on if the card was already tokenized or not. 4. If the card has not been tokenized, the CDE will kick off a job to asynchronously tokenize the credit card using Visa Token Service. 5. The card details are passed on to Visa Token Service and exchanged for a network token. 6. If the card has already been tokenized, the network token is fetched from Cardservers database. 7. A cryptogram is created for the network token by passing the network token to Visa Token Service. 8. The public key that the partner handed to Shopify has its certificate verified using the Hashicorp vault certificate authority which is who signed the partners CSR. The public key is also validated against Partner Certificate Permissions to ensure the partner and public key has permission to fetch encrypted card data. If all PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 45

this is valid, the data is encrypted using the public key. If the network token did not exist, (4, and 5) then the data being encrypted is card details. If the network token existed (6, and 7), then the data being encrypted is the payment bundle (a token/cryptogram pair). 9. The encrypted data is returned from Shopifys CDE to Shop Pay. 10. Shop Pay queries its database to fetch the billing and shopping address for the buyer. 11. The encrypted data, shipping, and billing address is returned to the partner. Payment for APay on Shopify This flow is triggered when a buyer selects APay/Buy with Prime as their payment method. 1.The buyer completes the checkout by clicking Pay now. The APay sdk from Amazon generates a checkout ID that is used in this flow. The sdk redirects the buyer to Amazon to login and select a payment method and redirects back to shopify with a checkout id. 2. Shopify sends the checkout id to Amazon to indicate that it wants to charge this credit card. 3. If the PAN exchange has not previously happened, Amazon will trigger it to block until it is complete. 4. Amazon returns a vault card token that was obtained during the APay PAN exchange flow. 5. Shopify passes the vault card token, along with checkout information to Cardserver to create a transaction. 6. Cardserver fetches the card from its datastore using the vault card token. The card is then decrypted. 7. Cardserver sends the PAN to the Payment Gateway for processing. 8. The Payment Gateway returns payment transaction information to Cardserver. This includes no cardholder data; however, it will include a truncated PAN. 9. Cardserver returns the transaction information to Shopify, and Shopify stores information about the transaction in its datastore. (no CHD) 10. The buyer is redirected to a successful/failed checkout flow depending on the result of the payment. Payment for Facebook Pay on Shopify 1. A buyer clicks pay; the JSON Web Token that was generated by Facebook is passed to Shopify Core and is stored to the checkouts table. The token was generated using a public key provided to Facebook by Shopify. 2. Shopify creates an order for the checkout. 3. Shopify creates a transaction by passing the token to Shopifys Card Data Environment for payment processing. 4. The Tokens signature is validated; if the signature is valid the token is decrypted using a private key. Cardholder data is fetched from the decrypted token and verifications on the transaction amount and token expiry are performed. 5. The cardholder data is passed to Stripe to process the payment. 6. The result of Stripe processing the payment is passed back to Shopify core, and Shopify core lets Facebook know if the payment was successful or not. Payment Partners Platform - Checkout This flow describes how third-party payments providers (payments partners) can process buyers credit card information for a purchase in Shopify checkout. Flow  Service Provider Dataflow After a buyer provides their credit card information in Hosted Fields, each cards data is sent to Cardsink which creates a Session for that card, identified by a Session ID. PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 46

This Session ID is then sent to Payments Platform in a request to process the payment. Payments Platform then requests the encrypted credit card information from Cardserver using the Session Id and the partners public key. Cardserver reads and deletes the card associated to the given Session ID from Memcached, verifies that the public key provided for encryption is valid (expiry, CA chain and CRL check) and encrypts the card information to send it back to Payments Platform. Once Payment Platform receives the encrypted data, it forwards it to the payments partner apps, where the partner can decrypt the payload with their private key and process the payments. This last step happens outside of Shopify infrastructure, in the partners PCI environment. Payment Partners Platform - Wallet This flow describes how third-party payments providers (payments partners) can process wallet paymentmethods Checkout Flow - Service Provider Dataflow from checkout. 1. A buyer initiates a checkout and selects a wallet as a payment method provider (Google Pay or Apple Pay) within, initiating the JavaScript API and displaying the payment method selection sheet. 2. Upon selection of a payment method, an Elliptic Curve Integrated Encryption Scheme (ECIES) encrypted payload containing the users chosen payment data is sent to Shopify. 3. This Wallet Payload is then sent to Payments Platform in a request to process the payment. 4. Payments Platform then requests the encrypted credit card information from Cardserver using the wallet payload and the partners public key. 5. The wallet payload is decrypted using Shopify credentials in accordance with the ECIES encryption scheme. Keys rotate yearly. 6. Cardserver verifies that the public key provided for partner encryption is valid (expiry, CA chain and CRLcheck) and encrypts the card information using the Payment App Partner Certificate using ECIES. 7. Once Payment Platform receives the encrypted data, it forwards it to the payments partner apps. 8. The partner can decrypt the payload with their private key and process the payments. This last step happens outside of Shopify infrastructure, in the partners PCI environment. Payments for Shop Pay on MarketPlace Facebook is processing payments for credit cards held in the Shop Pay wallet. The flow is triggered when a buyer Facebook - A Shop Pay user uses stored is purchasing a product from Facebook and the payment method they choose is Shop Pay. The buyer is payment method on a Facebook Merchant redirected to Shop Pay to login. Upon logging in and selecting their payment method, they are redirected back to shop (non-Shopify Merchant) Facebook. On the redirect back, Facebook is passed an access token. 1. Facebook calls the confirm order API with the access token that was obtained from the redirect, along with a public key that will be used for encryption. 2. Shop Pay maps the access token to a vaulted card token, calls into Shopifys Card Data Environment with the vault card token and the public key to fetch the card data. 3. The credit card is fetched from Shopifys datastore using the vaulted card token. 4. The public key that Facebook handed Shopify has its certificate verified using the Hashicorp vault certificate authority which is who signed Facebooks CSR. The public key is also validated against Partner Certificate PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 47

Permissions to ensure the partner and public key has permission to fetch encrypted card data. If all this is valid, the card data is encrypted using the public key. Otherwise, a validation error is raised resulting in a fail out of this flow. 5. The encrypted card data is returned from Shopifys CDE to Shop Pay. 6. Shop Pay queries its database to fetch the billing and shopping address for the buyer. 7. The encrypted card data, shipping, and billing address are returned to Facebook. Payment Partners Platform - Vaulted This flow describes how third-party payments providers (payments partners) can process vaulted cards from Payment Checkout Flow - Service Provider checkout. Dataflow 1. For a logged in customer account, their available cards on file are surfaced for checkout. 2. After a buyer has selected their vaulted payment method at checkout, it is forwarded to checkout one for payment processing. 3. The associated vault card record is located in core. From this the encrypted_vault_token stored on the record is loaded and decrypted. 4. This Vault Token is then sent to Payments Platform in a request to process the payment. 5. Payments Platform then requests the encrypted credit card information from Cardserver using the vault token and the partners public key. 6. Cardserver loads the vaulted credit card associated to the provided vault token. 7. Cardserver verifies that the public key provided for encryption is valid (expiry, CA chain and CRL check) and encrypts the card information to send it back to Payments Platform. 8. Once Payment Platform receives the encrypted data, it forwards it to the payments partner apps. 9. The partner can decrypt the payload with their private key and process the payments. This last step happens outside of Shopify infrastructure, in the partners PCI environment. POS Hosted Fields Manual Entry Dataflow This diagram is depicting the dataflow for a credit card payment in a Shopify checkout except for SP France - Service Provider Dataflow 1.The buyer adds items to a cart and navigates to the checkout page. 2. The credit card fields are retrieved from Hosted Fields and are rendered in iFrames in the checkout page. 2a. A check is made to see if the card number is valid using LUHN, as well as a check of the cards expiry. If either validation fails, the transaction does not move forward. 3. The PAN, CVV, expiry, and name are sent in a POST request to Cardsink. 3a. The posted data is encrypted using the public key. 3b. A session ID is generated which will be used to reference the encrypted data in Memcached. 4. The encrypted data is stored in Memcached and is referenced by the session ID. Data stored in Memcached expires after 1 hour. 5. The session ID is returned to the buyers browser. The session ID does not contain any cardholder data. 6. The session ID is sent in a POST request to Shopify 7. The session ID, along with the card and payment provider details are passed to Cardserver. 8. Cardserver fetches the encrypted card details from Memcached using the session ID. 8a. Cardserver decrypts the card details using the private key. 8b. Cardserver deletes the encrypted card details from Memcached, thus making session IDs single use. 9. The PAN, CVV, expiry and name are sent to the payment provider to create an auth. 10. A truncated PAN, status of auth and identifiers for the Auth are returned to Cardserver. PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 48

11. The truncated PAN, identifiers, and status of auth are returned to Shopify from the CDE. POS Dataflow Diagram  Service Provider Shopify powered card readers employ end-to-end encryption using DUKPT key management scheme. Dataflow 1.Shopify POS starts a checkout in Shopify App and activates the card reader for card entry. 2. The cardholder taps/inserts/swipes their card and completes any required prompts (i.e. account/application selection, cardholder PIN entry, etc.). The cardholder data is encrypted on the device and sent to Shopify POS. (encrypted cardholder data) 3. Shopify POS posts the encrypted cardholder data directly to Cardsink. (encrypted cardholder data) 4. Cardsink caches encrypted cardholder data in Memcache and returns a reference hash to Shopify POS. (encrypted cardholder data) 5. Shopify POS posts a transaction to Shopify App, using the reference hash, and Shopify App posts the transaction request to Cardserver. (no cardholder data) 6. Cardserver retrieves the encrypted cardholder data from Memcache and decrypts data. (cardholder data) 7. Cardserver posts the transaction to Payment Gateway. (cardholder data) 8. Cardserver receives transaction response, including auth  and receipt. (no cardholder data) 9. Response sent to Shopify App to store transaction response and auth . (no cardholder data) 10. Confirmation of capture transaction approval sent to POS and checkout is completed. (no cardholder data) POS Stripe Terminal Dataflow Diagram Stripe Terminal powered card readers employ end-to-end encryption using DUKPT key management scheme. (Delayed Capture enabled)  Service The Shopify POS app embeds Stripes Terminal SDK. The Stripe Terminal SDK is responsible for handling all Provider Dataflow communication to the card reader, as well as transmitting the encrypted cardholder data directly to Stripes servers. Stripes PCI Attestation of Compliance (AOC) covers the Stripe Terminal SDK and all transaction processing using Stripe Terminal powered card readers. 1. Shopify POS starts a checkout in Shopify App and activates the card reader for card entry. 2. The cardholder taps/inserts/swipes their card and completes any required prompts (i.e. account/application selection, cardholder PIN entry, etc.). The cardholder data is encrypted on the device and sent to Stripe Terminal SDK via Bluetooth connection. (encrypted cardholder data) 3. Stripe Terminal SDK posts the encrypted cardholder data directly to Stripes server and obtains authorization for transaction amount. (encrypted cardholder data) 4. Authorization response sent to Stripe Terminal SDK and subsequently forwarded to Shopify POS. (no cardholder data) 5. For approved authorizations, Shopify POS posts a capture request to Shopify App. (no cardholder data) 6. Shopify App posts a request to Cardserver to create transaction (no cardholder data) 7. Cardserver sends capture transaction request to Stripe. (no cardholder data) 8. Cardserver receives capture response. (no cardholder data) 9. Response sent to Shopify App to store transaction response and auth . (no cardholder data) 10. Confirmation of capture transaction approval sent to POS and checkout is completed. (no cardholder data) Vaulted Card  Checkout  Service This flow is triggered when buyer registered with Shop Pay is in checkout. Provider Dataflow 1.A buyer has items in cart, continues to checkout and enters their email/phone number. 2. The Browser calls Shop Pay to check if the email/phone number is already a registered account. PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 49

3. Shop pay sends an SMS to the phone number on file and has the buyer enter the code in the browser for verification. 4. Shop pay fetches the default payment method for the user and sends a POST request to Cardsink with the vault card token, as well as the shipping address. 5. Cardsink returns a session ID to Shop Pay. This is the same as the checkout flow. 6. Shop Pay returns to Shopify app and redirects the app to a checkout review page. 7. The buyer confirms the checkout. The Shopify app calls into Cardserver to process the payment. 8. The card details are fetched from Cardservers datastore and decrypted. 9. The card details are sent to the Payment Gateway for processing. 10. The response from processing the payment is returned to Cardserver. There is no cardholder data returned here. However, there is a truncated PAN. 11.The transaction details are returned to Shopify and stored. 12. The buyer is redirected to a failed/successful checkout page depending on the result of the payment. Visa Risk Manager  Redacting Proxy  This flow is triggered by clock job in Banking which generates, and queues calls to Visa Risk Manager via Dataflow Cardserver Proxy. 1. Job in banking prepares payload for Visa Risk Manager and sends it Cardserver Proxy. 2. Cardserver Proxy forwards the payload to Visa Risk Manager. 3. Visa Risk Manager responds with PCI adjacent data in response body. 4. Cardserver runs Redacting service on top of the response body to remove any PCI information. 5. This redacted response is sent to banking as response for further processing. Wharf (CCIE) - Card Import Vault Dataflow This flow describes how merchants import credit card data from a payment provider into Shopifys Cardholder - Service Provider Dataflow Data Environment and vaults the card data. Credit Card Importer Exporter (CCIE) is a service which runs inside Shopifys Cardholder Data Environment. The merchants payment provider prepares the data for export and encrypts the file with Shopifys public PGP key. This encrypted file is uploaded into Shopify Cardholder Data Environment, stored in Google Cloud Storage. After the file is uploaded, CCIE downloads the file and decrypts it to memory. Each cards data in the data file is sent to Cardsink which creates a Session for that card, identified by a Session ID. This Session ID is then sent to Cardserver in a request to vault the credit card, creating a credit card record and Vault Card record. The vault token identifying the Vault Card is returned to CCIE. Wharf (CCIE)  Card Exporter Flow, This flow describes how Shopify exports credit cards to new payment processors upon a merchants request. reviewed by Axel Gustav, 01/29/24 Credit Card Importer Exporter (CCIE) is a service which runs inside Shopifys Cardholder Data Environment. To process an export in the CCIE, the service will loop over all the cards associated with each customer to be exported. The CCIE has the vault token representing each card. Step 1: For each card, an API on Cardserver is called to retrieve all of the card details. PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 50

Step 2: Cardserver loads the card, and the card number is encrypted in the response using Rails ActiveSupport::MessageEncryptor. Step 3: CCIE decrypts the card number. Step 4: CCIE adds the card details to the customers information. Step 5: Once all of the information for a given customer has been accumulated, the customer data is encrypted using HashiCorp Vault transit engine. Step 6: The encrypted data is then stored in Memcache, using a Memcache hash. Once all the customers data has been accumulated, CCIE iterates over all the members of the Memcache hash: Step 7: Retrieving each customers data. Step 8: Decrypt this customers data. Step 9: accumulates it into one in-memory Json format data file. Step 10: Once complete, this data file is then encrypted using GPG with the public key of the receiving payment provider. Step 11: This encrypted file is stored in a Google Cloud Storage bucket. Step 12: The encrypted file is then retrieved from the Google Cloud Storage bucket, Step 13: The encrypted file is transmitted to the receiving payment provider using SFTP. 4.3 Storage of Account Data Identify all databases, tables, and files storing account data and provide the following details: Note: The list of files and tables that store account data in the table below must be supported by an inventory created (or obtained from the assessed entity) and retained by the assessor in the workpapers. Appendix B Compensating Controls Compensating controls may be considered when an entity cannot meet a PCI DSS requirement explicitly as stated, due to legitimate and documented technical or business constraints but has sufficiently mitigated the risk associated with not meeting the requirement through implementation of other, or compensating, controls. Compensating controls must satisfy the following criteria: 1. Meet the intent and rigor of the original PCI DSS requirement. 2. Provide a similar level of defense as the original PCI DSS requirement, such that the compensating control sufficiently offsets the risk that the original PCI DSS requirement was designed to defend against. To understand the intent of a requirement, see the Customized Approach Objective for most PCI DSS requirements. If a requirement is not eligible for the Customized Approach and therefore does not have a Customized Approach Objective, refer to the Purpose in the Guidance column for that requirement. 3. Be above and beyond other PCI DSS requirements. (Simply being in compliance with other PCI DSS requirements is not a compensating control.) 4. When evaluating above and beyond for compensating controls, consider the following: Note: All compensating controls must be reviewed and validated for sufficiency by the assessor who conducts the PCI DSS assessment. The effectiveness of a compensating control is dependent on the specifics of the environment in which the control is implemented, the surrounding security controls, and the configuration of the control. Entities should be aware that a given compensating control will not be effective in all environments. a. Existing PCI DSS requirements CANNOT be considered as compensating controls if they are already required for the item under review. For example, passwords for non-console administrative access must be sent encrypted to mitigate the risk of intercepting cleartext administrative passwords. An entity cannot use other PCI DSS password requirements (intruder lockout, complex passwords, etc.) to compensate for the lack of encrypted passwords, since those other password requirements do not mitigate the risk of interception of cleartext passwords. Also, the other password controls are already PCI DSS requirements for the item under review (passwords). b. Existing PCI DSS requirements MAY be considered as compensating controls if they are required for another area but are not required for the item under review. c. Existing PCI DSS requirements may be combined with new controls to become a compensating control. For example, if a company is unable to address a vulnerability that is exploitable through a network interface because a security update is not yet available from a vendor, a compensating control could consist of controls that include all of the following: 1) internal network segmentation, 2) limiting network access to the vulnerable interface to only required devices (IP address or MAC address filtering), and 3) IDS/IPS monitoring of all traffic destined to the vulnerable interface. PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 413

5. Address the additional risk imposed by not adhering to the PCI DSS requirement. 6. Address the requirement currently and in the future. A compensating control cannot address a requirement that was missed in the past (for example, where the performance of a task was required two quarters ago, but that task was not performed). The assessor is required to thoroughly evaluate compensating controls during each annual PCI DSS assessment to confirm that each compensating control adequately addresses the risk that the original PCI DSS requirement was designed to address, per items 1-6 above. To maintain compliance, processes and controls must be in place to ensure compensating controls remain effective after the assessment is complete. Additionally, compensating control results must be documented in the applicable report for the assessment (for example, a Report on Compliance or a Self-Assessment Questionnaire) in the corresponding PCI DSS requirement section, and included when the applicable report is submitted to the requesting organization. PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 414

Requirement Description 3.2 Storage of account data is kept to a minimum. PCI DSS Requirement 3.2.1 Account data storage is kept to a minimum through implementation of data retention and disposal policies, procedures, and processes that include at least the following:  Coverage for all locations of stored account data.  Coverage for any sensitive authentication data (SAD) stored prior to completion of authorization. This bullet is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required as part of Requirement 3.2.1 and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.  Limiting data storage amount and retention time to that which is required for legal or regulatory, and/or business requirements.  Specific retention requirements for stored account data that defines length of retention period and includes a documented business justification.  Processes for secure deletion or rendering account data unrecoverable when no longer needed per the retention policy.  A process for verifying, at least once every three months, that stored account data exceeding the defined retention period has been securely deleted or rendered unrecoverable. PCI DSS Requirement 3.5.1 PAN is rendered unreadable anywhere it is stored by using any of the following approaches:  One-way hashes based on strong cryptography of the entire PAN.  Truncation (hashing cannot be used to replace the truncated segment of PAN).  If hashed and truncated versions of the same PAN, or different truncation formats of the same PAN, are present in an environment, additional controls are in place such that the different versions cannot be correlated to reconstruct the original PAN.  Index tokens.  Strong cryptography with associated key-management processes and procedures. Appendix C Compensating Controls Worksheet Use this worksheet to document any instance where a compensating control is used to meet a PCI DSS defined requirement. Note that compensating controls must also be documented at the corresponding PCI DSS requirement in Part II, section 7 Findings and Observations. Note: Only entities that have legitimate and documented technological or business constraints can consider the use of compensating controls to achieve compliance. Requirement Number and Definition: 8.3.6 If passwords/passphrases are used as authentication factors to meet Requirement 8.3.1, they meet the following minimum level of complexity:  A minimum length of 12 characters (or IF the system does not support 12 characters, a minimum length of eight characters).  Contain both numeric and alphabetic characters. Information Required Explanation 1. Constraints Document the legitimate technical or business Business constraints: constraints precluding compliance with the original requirement.  Shopify uses Okta as Identity Provider and all essential systems and services including the CDE systems are behind Okta authentication. Shopify has had an 8-character minimum password requirement for Okta for many years. This requirement applies to 10,000 Shopify employees. Increasing the password length requirement causes mass forced password updates which does not necessarily decrease the risk.  Enforcing changes to longer passwords introduces a new potential risk of users writing down their passwords or unsafely storing them electronically because new longer passwords are less likely to be memorable.  To memorize their password, some users tend to follow predictable patterns or reuse longer passwords they have for their other accounts which gives a false sense of security. However, its a risk if any of the previous secrets have been compromised since attackers can apply these same common transformations. Therefore, Shopify keeps the password minimum length requirement to 8 characters. PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 415

2. Definition of Define the compensating controls, explain how Shopify uses context-based access (for authentication and authorization) Compensating they address the objectives of the original based on the BeyondCorp approach championed by Google along with Controls control and the increased risk, if any. hardware based multi-factor authentication. Context-based access is a security model that dynamically adjusts permissions by evaluating real-time factors such as user identity, device security, location, and compliance requirements to control access to resources. Once the necessary factors are evaluated, the system actively controls access by either granting or denying it based on the fulfillment of predefined conditions. This is also sometimes referred to as ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control) We enforce context-based access via Minerva. Minerva is a homegrown tool. Minerva is an authentication and authorization proxy that is used to dynamically assess access requirements. Here is a summary of compensating controls that in practice are in alignment with NIST SP 800-63 Section 5.1.1.2 guidance: - Restricting use of common passwords through Okta. - Enforcing account lockout to prevent brute force attacks. - Securely identifying the device - via managed laptop - Securely identifying the user - Removing Trust from the Network - Having a secure memorable password. - Hardware backed MFA to be phishing resistant. - JIT (Just In Time) access via Clouddo Policies - Github access IP allow-listing via Cloudflare WARP 3. Objective Define the objective of the original control (for A guessed password/passphrase cannot be verified by either an online or example, the Customized Approach Objective). offline brute force attack. Identify the objective met by the compensating The objective of the compensating control is to implement layers of defense control (note: this can be, but is not required to to lower the risk of unauthorized access to Shopify systems while reducing be, the stated Customized Approach Objective the user experience friction and sole reliance on password strength. for the PCI DSS requirement). A Brute force attack is not possible because the account lockout policy is in place. Even if an Okta password is guessed, it cannot be used to access any CDE system without possession of an authorized Shopify laptop, physical MFA device, and Clouddo authorization. PCI DSS Requirement 10.6.2 Systems are configured to the correct and consistent time as follows:  One or more designated time servers are in use.  Only the designated central time server(s) receives time from external sources.  Time received from external sources is based on International Atomic Time or Coordinated Universal Time (UTC).  The designated time server(s) accept time updates only from specific industry-accepted external sources.  Where there is more than one designated time server, the time servers peer with one another to keep accurate time.  Internal systems receive time information only from designated central time server(s). PCI DSS Requirement 10.7.3 Failures of any critical security control systems are responded to promptly, including but not limited to:  Restoring security functions. PCI DSS Requirement 12.5.2 PCI DSS scope is documented and confirmed by the entity at least once every 12 months and upon significant change to the in-scope environment. At a minimum, the scoping validation includes:  Identifying all data flows for the various payment stages (for example, authorization, capture settlement, chargebacks, and refunds) and acceptance channels (for example, card-present, card-not-present, and e-commerce).  Updating all data-flow diagrams per Requirement 1.2.4.  Identifying all locations where account data is stored, processed, and transmitted, including but not limited to: 1) any locations outside of the currently defined CDE, 2) applications that process CHD, 3) transmissions between systems and networks, and 4) file backups.  Identifying all system components in the CDE, connected to the CDE, or that could impact security of the CDE.  Identifying all segmentation controls in use and the environment(s) from which the CDE is segmented, including justification for environments being out of scope.  Identifying all connections from third-party entities with access to the CDE.  Confirming that all identified data flows, account data, system components, segmentation controls, and connections from third parties with access to the CDE are included in scope. Appendix D Customized Approach This approach is intended for entities that decide to meet a PCI DSS requirements stated Customized Approach Objective in a way that does not strictly follow the defined requirement. The customized approach allows an entity to take a strategic approach to meeting a requirements Customized Approach Objective, so it can determine and design the security controls needed to meet the objective in a manner unique for that organization. The entity implementing a customized approach must satisfy the following criteria:  Document and maintain evidence about each customized control, including all information specified in the Controls Matrix Template in PCI DSS v4.x: Sample Templates to Support Customized Approach on the PCI SSC website.  Perform and document a targeted risk analysis (PCI DSS Requirement 12.3.2) for each customized control, including all information specified in the Targeted Risk Analysis Template in PCI DSS v4.x: Sample Templates to Support Customized Approach on the PCI SSC website.  Perform testing of each customized control to prove effectiveness, and document testing performed, methods used, what was tested, when testing was performed, and results of testing in the controls matrix.  Monitor and maintain evidence about the effectiveness of each customized control.  Provide completed controls matrix(es), targeted risk analysis, testing evidence, and evidence of customized control effectiveness to its assessor. The assessor performing an assessment of customized controls must satisfy the following criteria:  Review the entitys controls matrix(es), targeted risk analysis, and evidence of control effectiveness to fully understand the customized control(s) and to verify the entity meets all Customized Approach documentation and evidence requirements.  Derive and document the appropriate testing procedures needed to conduct thorough testing of each customized control.  Test each customized control to determine whether the entitys implementation 1) meets the requirements Customized Approach Objective and 2) results in an in place finding for the requirement.  At all times, QSAs maintain independence requirements defined in the QSA Qualification Requirements. This means if a QSA is involved in designing or implementing a customized control, that QSA does not also derive testing procedures for, assess, or assist with the assessment of that customized control. The entity and its assessor are expected to work together to ensure 1) they agree that the customized control(s) fully meets the customized approach objective, 2) the assessor fully understands the customized control, and 3) the entity understands the derived testing the assessor will perform. PCI DSS v4.0.1 ROC Template r3 January 2025 Copyright  20062025 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All rights reserved. Page 419

Use of the customized approach must be documented by a QSA or ISA in accordance with instructions in the Report on Compliance (ROC) Template and following the instructions in the FAQs for use with PCI DSS v4.x ROC Template available on the PCI SSC website. Entities that complete a Self-Assessment Questionnaire are not eligible to use a customized approach; however, these entities may elect to have a QSA or ISA perform their assessment and document it in a ROC Template. The use of the customized approach may be regulated by organizations that manage compliance programs (for example, payment brands and acquirers). Therefore, questions about use of a customized approach must be referred to those organizations, including, for example, whether an entity is required to use a QSA, or may use an ISA to complete an assessment using the customized approach. Note: Compensating controls are not an option with the customized approach. Because the customized approach allows an entity to determine and design the controls needed to meet a requirements Customized Approach Objective, the entity is expected to effectively implement the controls it designed for that requirement without needing to also implement alternate, compensating controls. Appendix E Customized Approach Template Use this template to document each instance where a customized control is used to meet a PCI DSS requirement. Note that each use of the Customized Approach must also be documented at the corresponding PCI DSS requirement in Part II, section 7 Findings and Observations. Requirement Number and Definition: Identify the customized control name / identifier for each control used to meet the Customized Approach Objective. (Note: use the Customized Control name from the assessed entitys controls matrix) Describe each control used to meet the Customized Approach Objective. (Note: Refer to the Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Requirements and Testing Procedures for the Customized Approach Objective) Describe how the control(s) meet the Customized Approach Objective. Identify the Controls Matrix documentation reviewed that supports a customized approach for this requirement. Identify the Targeted Risk Analysis documentation reviewed that supports the customized approach for this requirement. Identify name(s) of the assessor(s) who attests that:  The entity completed the Controls Matrix including all information specified in the Controls Matrix Template in PCI DSS v4.x: Sample Templates to Support Customized Approach on the PCI SSC website, and the results of the Controls Matrix support the customized approach for this requirement.  The entity completed the Targeted Risk Analysis including all information specified in the Targeted Risk Analysis Template in PCI DSS v4.x: Sample Templates to Support Customized Approach on the PCI SSC website, and that the results of the Risk Analysis support use of the customized approach for this requirement. Describe the testing procedures derived and performed by the assessor to validate that the implemented controls meet the Customized Approach Objective; for example, whether the customized control(s) is sufficiently robust to provide at least an equivalent level of protection as provided by the defined approach. Note 1: Technical reviews (for example, reviewing configuration settings, operating effectiveness, etc.) should be performed where possible and appropriate. Note 2: Add additional rows for each assessor-derived testing procedure, as needed. Ensure that all rows to the right of the Assessor-derived testing procedure are copied for each assessor-derived testing procedure that is added. Enter assessor-derived testing Identify what was tested (for procedure here: example, individuals interviewed, system components reviewed, processes observed, etc.) Note: all items tested must be uniquely identified. Identify all evidence examined for this testing procedure. Describe the results of the testing performed by the assessor for this testing procedure and how these results verify the implemented controls meet the Customized Approach Objective.
